channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - techraptor.net
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for techraptor.net

item.title: Halloween Preview: Breathing New Life Into a Horror Classic
item.content:encoded:
At PAX East, we got the opportunity to go hands-on with Halloween, the new asymmetric survival multiplayer game releasing this fall from IllFonic. Based on the cinematic horror classic, Halloween brilliantly brings in elements straight out of the film while offering players some new exciting mechanics to toy with.

With a trusted pedigree that includes titles like Friday the 13th: The Game and Predator: Hunting Grounds, IllFonic is no stranger to creating engaging asymmetrical multiplayer experiences. While those playing as civilians need to hide, find weapons, and convince fellow townspeople to help, the one playing Michael Myers must stalk, master a selection of abilities, and use stealth to take down the other players.

During my Halloween preview, I had the opportunity to play as both a civilian and as Michael Myers. Through both perspectives one thing became brutally clear: Nobody is safe when Michael Myers is on the loose.

Halloween Preview: Nailing Late '70s Horror Atmosphere

During my Halloween demo, we had the opportunity to revisit a familiar locale straight out of the film: Michael's neighborhood in Haddonfield, IL. IllFonic Design Director Jordan Mathewson told me how they were able to recreate the iconic neighborhood, allowing players to visit both Laurie Strode's home and the Myers homestead.

"Halloween is such an iconic franchise with one of the most iconic killers out there," Mathewson said. "A lot of what makes it is recreating that idyllic Midwestern town that you then put this killer into. We looked at the movie, and then had to translate that into this sandbox multiplayer experience to see what players go and do with it."

I was able to explore the neighborhood, hide in cars, lock doors, and try to recruit NPCs to help. Starting a conversation with an NPC will trigger a series of QTEs that, when done successfully, can help pull that character onto the side of the survivors. You can then give them a job, like hide or call the police, to improve your odds of survival. Having the cops show up can be a helpful way to get Michael under control, but just like in the film, never trust when you think he's dead.

While playing as Michael, a police officer took me down with several shots from his handgun, but there was only a short recovery period before I was right back up and back to killing again. While Michael stalks with his signature slow walk that you can slightly speed up, the slasher has a variety of abilities that give him critical advantages over those trying to escape their nightmare.

Halloween Preview: Michael Myers Gameplay & Mechanics 

As an asymmetric multiplayer game, Halloween has to strike that balance of advantages Michael has over the survivors and vice versa. While Michael walks at his slow pace, he has a Shape Jump ability that allows him to essentially go invisible and float through the world at high speeds, making it easier to appear suddenly behind an unsuspecting player.

"We couldn't just do another Jason or another Predator," IllFonic Chief Creative Officer Jared Gerritzen said. "So we figured out this Shape Jump ability. Michael can just disappear and reappear anywhere. It's what makes Michael feel exactly like Michael in the film. He's a slow-coiled snake. So once Michael got figured out, everything else fell into place."

Shape Jump may have been the first ability the team worked out, it's just one of tools that Michael Myers has accessible to get a competitive edge over his victims: a killer sense where you look through the mask and can spot sounds that other players are making, quick and slow knife attacks to bloody the civilians, and even abilities that affect the environment.

"He has a blackout ability," Mathewson said. "He can shut down power manually and cut the lights to houses, but he also has an ability to cut all the power in a particular area."

Plunging multiple homes into darkness at once enhances the classic horror atmosphere of this game, and to make sure the tone stayed true to the original, the team worked closely with the creator himself.

"We have meetings with John Carpenter," Gerritzen said. "The first time we met him, he was like, 'This thing is yours. I've been working on this forever, I've done everything I want to do with it.'

"The story is talking about the parts that you don't see in the film. They allowed us to write this kind of history narrative that made it possible to do this."

The collaboration isn't without pressure, though. As Gerritzen said, "They're letting us do what we want, but John Carpenter is a gamer, he's definitely going to play it. And that scares the shit out of me." 

Halloween Release Date & Final Thoughts

The Halloween release date is currently set for September 8, 2026, perfectly timed to coincide with the season from which it takes its name. While my Halloween preview only focused on a single neighborhood, Mathewson and Gerritzen assured me that the world will be expanding, and a story mode will be available as well.

Halloween is shaping up to be a fun yet stressful asymmetric survival game that will pit stalker against prey, murderer against survivor. Players will need to execute every option in their toolbox to make it through the night alive, or else succumb to Michael's blade like so many before.

IllFonic is bringing their well-earned expertise to this project, and I had a great time exploring Haddonfield and seeing the little details that were included throughout. From discovering a TV playing Nosferatu inside one of the NPC houses (had to intentionally NOT spend my whole preview watching Nosferatu to be honest), to finding bicycles and items that civilians can pick up to help get away from Michael.

Players can expect to see Halloween launching on Xbox Series S/X, PS5 and PC this fall.

Halloween was previewed on a PC at PAX East 2026.

Dan is a Boston-based writer who has been with TechRaptor since the end of 2020. He has been working in the online writing, editing and SEO space for nearly… More about Dan



https://techraptor.net/gaming/previews/halloween-preview-breathing-new-life-into-horror-classic
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Eidos Montreal Lays Off Over 100 Employees, Studio Head Leaves
item.content:encoded:
The hits keep coming to the video game industry, as layoffs strike at yet another storied developer. Today, Eidos Montreal announced that they are laying off 124 employees, and that head of studio David Anfossi will also be departing.

In their announcement, Eidos Montreal states the layoffs are due to "changing project needs", and "impacts across production and support teams." The goal is to focus their efforts where Eidos Montreal can be "most effective". The company elaborated a bit more:

We are deeply grateful to the team members impacted; this decision is not a reflection of their talent, dedication, or performance. Supporting those impacted with care and respect remains our priority, while ensuring continuity for the teams moving forward.

Also, after many years as Head of Studio, David Anfossi and Eidos Montréal are also parting ways. We thank David for his contributions and wish him the best in his future endeavors. A transition plan is underway, and further updates will be shared as new leadership is finalized.

Eidos Montreal was founded in 2007, and it's first release was 2011's Deus Ex: Human Revolution, which was a celebrated return to the series. It would go on to work with Crystal Dynamics (who have also struggled with layoffs) on the Tomb Raider games before developing Shadow of the Tomb Raider to close out that trilogy.

Since then, they created the largely underrated Marvel's Guardians of the Galaxy and then went to work on a new Deus Ex game (which was cancelled, followed by several rounds of layoffs). Following that they have been doing co-development work on Grounded 2 with Obsidian, and on the upcoming Fable from Playground Studios.

Layoffs have been hitting the industry a lot over the last few years, and seemingly without any connection to success of projects as we see developers working on hit games like Battlefield 6, and Fortnite getting laid off en-masse.

A longtime lover of speculative fiction, in almost all its forms, Don joined TechRaptor in 2014 on a whim sending in an application as he was looking for… More about Don



https://techraptor.net/gaming/news/eidos-montreal-lays-off-over-100-employees-studio-head-leaves
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Riot Games Announces First Riftbound Ban List for League of Legends TCG
item.content:encoded:
Riot Games has announced its first official ban list for its League of Legends TCG. The first Riftbound ban list will restrict the use of several cards in the game's Standard Constructed format. In addition, the developers have articulated their reasons for their first ban.

The First Riftbound Ban List Announcement

According to an official Riot Games blog post, the first Riftbound ban list will ban the following cards and battlefields from Constructed play effect March 31, 2026.

  • Called Shot
  • Draven, Vanquisher
  • Fight or Flight
  • Scrapheap
  • Battlefields
  • Disarming Tree
  • Obelisk of Power
  • Reaver's Row

The rest of the blog post is dedicated to the reasoning behind this ban list. It opens stating that the ban list is a work in progress. This is to be expected with shifting competitive metas in any card game, be it Riftbound or Magic: The Gathering. 

The guiding principles behind the Riftbound ban list are summarized into several parts. These include questions about the impact of the card. Is the card unhealthily overrepresented? Will the cards' problems get worse over time? Does the card promote play patterns that negatively impact the game?

The other parts are more of a design philosophy to which  the team is maintaining. One is not changing the game's core rules or just changing the card's effects via errata. The other is outright banning cards instead of restricting how many copies can be in a deck.

This is for several reasons. One is if the developers starting changing rules now, it would be devastating to the long-term health of Riftbound, especially when each new set like Vendetta introduces new gameplay mechanics. The second is banning cards rather than restricting them prevents the problem getting worse through dedicated magnified randomness.

The final part of the blog post goes into the exact details behind the banned cards. Called Shot and Scrapheap are banned since they are part of what's called "Miracle Decks" in the community, which lets players draw and play large numbers of cards at discounted costs. Draven is banned due to him being a power outlier, an outright mistake according to the developers.

Fight or Flight was banned to limit the power of Chaos decks. As for battlefields, Reaver's Row is banned due to its synergy with Draven and due to it encouraging noninteractive play patterns. Dreaming Tree provides substantial card flow, which can easily tip the scale towards certain deck styles. Lastly, Obelisk of Power is banned due to its ubiquity in competitive decks.

Ever since he was small, Tyler Chancey has had a deep, abiding love for video games and a tendency to think and overanalyze everything he enjoyed. This… More about Tyler



https://techraptor.net/tabletop/news/riot-games-announces-first-riftbound-ban-list-for-league-of-legends-tcg
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
